insight: Go PoC organized by `//go:build stepN` tags must be built against every declared tag after relocation, not just step1 — each tag selects a different import subset (verified poc/go-quic-wt: 6 tags; step1/2/5/6 ~10MB pull quic-go, step3/dump ~8MB do not).

polyforge:init --force regenerates ALL three files (workspace.yaml, .env.dev.example, CLAUDE.md) from the generic template — clobbering any user-edited workspace.yaml; to self-heal only missing files, run plain polyforge:init (MODE=repair, v0.11.3+).

polyforge:doc-init writes the wiki scaffold to .repo/<doc>/ on main (the canonical clone), not into the ticket worktree — run it BEFORE the first polyforge:up of any ticket that depends on the scaffold, otherwise you end up with a 2-PR ordering dance.

In polyforge multi-repo workspaces, shared docs (specs/plans/retros) MUST prefix every repo-relative path with the repo name (e.g. 'tether/poc/go-quic-wt/...') — bare repo-root-relative paths become ambiguous after the single→multi-repo split.
gh pr create silently fails when the active gh account differs from the repo owner; on multi-account hosts, verify 'gh auth status' shows the right 'Active account' before commit/PR flows.

<ticket-dir>/.polyforge.exceptions.log is the canonical audit trail for M-1 / M2 / M5 commit-gate skips, with explicit reasons — useful for any doc-only or build-only ticket where the standard verification/attestation gates do not apply.

Doc-only or trivially mechanical tickets can legitimately run with just .ticket-context.md (no .specs/ / .plans/ / .progress.md); polyforge:up does not require the full spec/plan loop, and skipping it for mechanical work is honest, not lazy.

Even byte-identical-source migrations need explicit DoD verification (build, tests) — 'source unchanged' is NOT the same as 'behavior preserved' because go.mod context, build-tag selection, or import paths can shift silently with relocation.

When introducing a workspace-wide rule alongside content the rule applies to, land the rule in a precursor PR; bundling lets 'do not modify content' justify shipping the same PR with rule-violations still in it.

wiki/SKILL.md is generated by polyforge:doc-init from wiki-skill-template.md in the polyforge plugin; any local additions (cross-repo refs rule, immutability exception) live only in this clone and will be reverted by polyforge:doc-init --force. Backlog: open a polyforge plugin PR to upstream these into wiki-skill-template.md so every workspace gets them on bootstrap.

When a downstream retro surfaces multiple upstream plugin / tooling improvements (architecture, UX, FR), file ONE umbrella tracking issue on the upstream repo with each item as a numbered section — easier for the maintainer to triage and assign, provides cohesive context for any follow-up agent — rather than fragmenting into one-issue-per-item.

For vendor-CLI integration where wire schema may evolve: parse a minimal common Envelope (Type, Subtype, Raw bytes) eagerly, expose specific DecodeX methods that consumers call on demand. Forward-compat falls out: unknown types pass through without aborting the stream. Used in tether's claude package; broadly applicable to any third-party NDJSON / event-stream wrapper.

One Session.Recover() method covers plugin-reload, pod-restart, daemon-crash, WT-reconnect — four callers, one primitive. Constraints: state machine + safe-to-reload gate (refuse non-idle states unless stuck >threshold) + AbortPending to drain in-flight outbound requests + atomic internals replacement under mutex. Pattern is backend-agnostic and applicable to any subprocess-wrapping daemon.

For lib packages that need contract verification against a real backend: a single cmd/spike binary with -scenarios=N flag + per-scenario PASS/FAIL/SKIP reporting + markdown report generation. Each scenario is a self-contained function exercising one contract end-to-end. Cheaper than full integration test fleet; higher signal than smoke test.

For 'absence of X' contracts (e.g. 'tether must never write cc's jsonl', 'must not depend on fd 3'): a Go test that scans source files for forbidden API+target-pair combinations is cheaper and tighter than a runtime check. Cannot drift, runs on every CI go-test invocation. Runtime cost ~0ms.

Solo coding without external reviewer leaves real bugs even with TDD discipline. Round-2 review on gh-13's implementation found 13 findings (7 race/panic/leak class) that the author missed during self-review. Pattern: dispatch parallel subagents — concurrency-focused + error-path/coverage-focused — at major milestones. ~5-10 min wall-clock, ~/bin/bash cost. Worth standardizing pre-PR.

Per-call cost is dominated by cache_creation token volume, not the model's per-token rate. Counter-example: Haiku 4.5 cost $0.111 vs Sonnet 4.6 $0.051 for the same prompt — Haiku's gstack SessionStart hook context dumped 74k cache_creation tokens vs Sonnet's 12k. Don't reach for Haiku-as-cheap intuition without measuring; compare actual usage tables.

Build scenario 1 (smallest end-to-end happy path) BEFORE writing parser/control/state-machine subsystems. The 'system/init only after first user msg' constraint surfaced as a Step 5 integration test failure in gh-13; if scenario 1 had been the first thing built, this would have been a Step 1 finding and saved one round of API redesign. Generalize: smoke-test-the-spawn-call before generalizing.

Run go test -race ./... on every commit, not just at review time. Caught the C2 race (Send/SendInterrupt/terminateAndWait reading s.sub without mu) post-implementation; would have been a Step 5 fix instead of merge-prep churn. The race detector is essentially free (~30% slowdown) and concurrency bugs are nearly invisible without it.
